# Reuse

The simplest use of `dhall-hpack-cabal` is to injest one self-contained
`package.dhall` file but by using imports and functions we can do more than just
create a single output, the `.cabal` file.

The trick with reuse is to break apart `package.dhall` into sections for reuse.

Section reuse is a facility Cabal has with its [common][cabal-common] stanzas
that can be imported by other common stanzas and by components in the package
description `.cabal` file. Cabal restricts what can be commonly shared to [build
information][cabal-buildinfo]. That's a lot of fields but there are also a lot
of fields that are excluded, such as the author and maintainer, copyright and
license-related fields.

[cabal-common]: https://cabal.readthedocs.io/en/latest/cabal-package-description-file.html#common-stanzas
[cabal-buildinfo]: https://cabal.readthedocs.io/en/latest/cabal-package-description-file.html#build-information

Section reuse is a facility YAML has too with its `&` anchors, `*` aliases, and
`<<` merge keys.

[hpack-reuse]: https://github.com/sol/hpack#not-repeating-yourself

What neither Cabal's `.cabal` files nor standard YAML `.yaml` files have is the
ability to include or import from another file but Hpack's [facilities for
reuse][hpack-reuse] support the non-standard YAML `!include` directive by its
use of [`Data.Yaml.Include.decodeFileWithWarnings`][data-yaml-include], a
function that does file inclusions by raw text insertion into the stream without
validation.

[data-yaml-include]: https://github.com/snoyberg/yaml/blob/7380d7f560daa2f45ff265d425866f497ca07966/yaml/src/Data/Yaml/Include.hs#L86-L94

## Dhall Import Advantages

Dhall's imports are great. They provide strongly typed, secure, and composable
configuration and have a lot of [saftey guarantees][dhall-safety].  Dhall
imports have (or optionally can have):

- **Type Checking Across Files**  Imports are type-checked before evaluation and
  it is possible to mark imported data with a separate imported type, something
  other data formats achieve with schemas.

- **Security**  It is possible to `dhall freeze`, which computes a SHA256 hash
  of the imported content.

- **Programmable Composition**  Imports are expressions. You can import a
  function and apply it to data or import data and pass it into a function.

- **Termination**  Dhall is not Turing-complete. Importing will never hang or
  crash the parser.

[dhall-safety]: https://docs.dhall-lang.org/discussions/Safety-guarantees.html

## Author and Maintainer Details

If a single individual or company is authoring and maintaining a lot of packages
then they might like to define their author and maintainers details once and
import those. They might put all their source code under the same GitHub
organisation and always use the same license but put each package in its own
repository and be prepared for changing copyright years.

```{.dhall filename=author.dhall}
\(copyright-years : Text) ->
  \(repo : Text) ->
      { author = "Thaddeus Blackwood"
      , maintainer = "thad@black-wood.invalid"
      , copyright = "© ${copyright-years} Thaddeus Blackwood"
      , github = "thad-black-wood/${repo}"
      , license = "MPL-2.0"
      , license-file = "LICENSE.md"
      , extra-source-files = [ "LICENSE.md" ]
      }
```

## Dual Targets

If the default extensions are in their own `default-extensions.dhall` file, then
we can generate a `.yaml` file equivalent and use this for configuring the
extensions that HLint turns on when linting.

```{.dhall filename=default-extensions.dhall}
{ default-extensions =
    [ "DataKinds"
    , "DeriveFunctor"
    ...
    , "TupleSections"
    , "UndecidableInstances"
    ]
}
```

We want to generate a `.yaml` file with an arguments list of extensions. The
Dhall function to do this is but a few lines long. It prefixes each extension
with "-X":

```{.dhall filename=hlint.dhall}
let Prelude/List/map =
          https://raw.githubusercontent.com/dhall-lang/Prelude/35deff0d41f2bf86c42089c6ca16665537f54d75/List/map

in  let defs = ./default-extensions.dhall

in  let f = λ(s : Text) → "-X" ++ s

in  { arguments = Prelude/List/map Text Text f defs.default-extensions }
```

::: {.callout-note collapse="false"}
Imports can be URIs or local files. The example above has both kinds.

Relative imports have to start with "./" or "../".
:::

We generate an `.hlint.yaml` file (or you could create an
`.hlint-extensions.yaml` file and import it) with `dhall-to-yaml < ./hlint.dhall
> ./.hlint.yaml`.

```{.yaml filename=.hlint.yaml}
arguments:
- -XDataKinds
- -XDeriveFunctor
# ...
- -XTupleSections
- -XUndecidableInstances
```

We can pull those same `default-extensions` into a package description:

```{.dhall filename=package.dhall}
let defs = ./defaults.dhall

in    defs
    ⫽ ./default-extensions.dhall
    ⫽ { name = "flight-units"
      , github = "blockscope/flare-timing/units"
      , dependencies =
            defs.dependencies
          # [ "numbers"
            , "fixed"
            , "bifunctors"
            , "text"
            , "formatting"
            , "uom-plugin"
            , "siggy-chardust"
            ]
      }
```

It doesn't really matter for this example what `defaults.dhall` contains except
that it too has dependencies. If it has its own `default-extensions` then those
are merged with `⫽`:

```{.dhall filename=defaults.dhall}
{ default-extensions =
    [ "PackageImports" ]
, dependencies =
    [ "base >=4.10.1.0 && <5" ]
}
```
